howItWorks.intro: This guitar string tension calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Tension (lbs) = (UW x (2 x Scale Length x Frequency)^2) / 386.4
Unit Weight = String Constant x (Gauge x 1000)^2
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What is ideal guitar string tension?
faq.answer: Most players prefer 14-18 lbs per string. Lower tension (10-14 lbs) is easier for bending. Higher tension (18-25 lbs) gives more volume and sustain but requires more finger strength.

faq.question: How does scale length affect tension?
faq.answer: Longer scale length increases tension at the same tuning. A Fender 25.5-inch scale has higher tension than a Gibson 24.75-inch scale with identical strings and tuning, which is why Gibson-scale guitars feel slinkier.

faq.question: Is the Guitar String Tension Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard music and audio formulas trusted by musicians and audio engineers. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What string gauge should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate string gauge value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Guitar String Tension Calculator?
sections.body: The Guitar String Tension Calculator is a free online tool designed for musicians and audio engineers who need quick, accurate calculations in the music and audio space. By entering your string gauge, scale length, target frequency, you get instant results including string tension, unit weight, tension rating. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting string tension right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In music and audio, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by musicians and audio engineers worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to fine-tune your sound with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your string gauge and need to find the right string tension. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your String Gauge (inches)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your string gauge — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: scale length, target frequency, string type. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your string tension, unit weight, tension rating. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your string gauge before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both string tension and unit weight — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to fine-tune your sound.
If you're unsure about your string type, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
